author: @guytech7310
text:
Unfortunatley none of the fusion methods are viable:

1. Magnetic confinment: Hot Plasma creates its own magnetic field that destabilizes the magnetic containment field. The longest run to maintain confinement is just a few minutes. it gets even worse because the fusion will create a lot of fast neutrons that will break down the materials used in superconductive magnets. Basically this method is a dead end.
2. Electrostatic confinement will simply never generate enough fusion reactions to product a lot of excessive energy. The issue is that all of the fusion reactions occur at a tiny point, making it impossible to scale up
3. Lattice confinement is limited in the rate of fusion reactions. Its will only produce fusion reactions very slowly. Consider like trying trying to travel across a contient at a snails pace. It Simply will never produce enough power to run turbines.
4. Laser Confinement. This is really just a nuclear weapons program to create pure fusion bombs with the need for any fissile material. This would provide militaries to produce small nuclear munitions without the fallout and bridge the gap between conventional weapons and fissile nuclear weapons. These would be use for bunker busters and other hardened targets. It's not pratical for power generation because it extremely difficult to get all of the laser beams and the pellet aligned to initial fusion.

Seems like the only viable sustainable fusion process is with gravitational confinement which cannot be replicated on a planet. You need about 0.075 solar masses to initial fusion which is about 25,000 times the mass of the earth.

Back when I was employed as a researcher in a university electrical engineering/engineering physics lab, I recall only too well the furor over the F-P cold fusion announcement. This was pre-World Wide Web, and in fact all the actual information we gleaned came in the form of copies of faxes that were on about their 37th re-transmission. This, plus the fax machine technology of the early 1990's, gave us a great deal of almost illegible and chopped up handwritten descriptions, sketches of experimental test rigs and so on. As it happened, my boss in the EE department was himself a physicist - he'd come over from England after studying with one of Britain's "Manhatten Project" physicists for his doctorate. He had a "rule of thumb" that everyone working in the lab should spend at least 25% of their time on their own ideas or projects - whatever those might be, which was a very clever and very refreshing environment for encouraging original thought. Anyhow, it turned out we had on hand, or had access to all of the materials set out in one "semi-complete" description of the F-P experiment. So of course, we all said, "Well, it sounds absurd but let's "suck it and see" for ourselves. So, we put together something that resembled the best of the vague & poorly-copied sketches of the lab rig. We had quite a lot of test equipment of all kinds on hand - it was that kind of a place, an Aladdin's Cave - so we were also able to set up various detectors and measuring instruments. This perked along for some weeks in "spare time" and after a while we had seen no signs of anything as described in the 'samizdat" pre-publication research papers. There was a good cross-section of skills among our lab researchers, and if there had been any results at all we'd probably have detected them. Very soon, however, we began to suspect that a) there was some piece of go/no-go information missing, or b) the whole thing was probably hooey. History will decide which of those two ideas was correct, LOL. In any event, after a while we decided our energies might best be put to other uses and other experimental problems, of which there was no shortage. 
We were very fortunate, I think, not only in having equipment and materials available, but in having a research group and group leader who did not write off the idea, or as much as we'd learned of it, as not reproduceable. We had taken on some other quite unusual projects, and some of those (to everyone's surprise) had actually led to genuine results. So, there was some freedom to test the unusual, and the knowledge that occasionally ideas that seemed very odd turned out to be have some basis. I've seen numerous examples of the "con-" variety of fusion, as well as the NASA experimental reports. I hope that something useful may yet come of it. Thanks for your great overview and summing up of the "state of the art" today.
I was reminded several months back of a stationary wind-powered generator that was another "blue sky" project. It essentially combined a Helmholtz resonator and piezo-electric device. The wind-powered Helmholtz resonator resulted from an undergraduate research paper I'd had to research years before, starting from the physics of clarinets, saxophones, flutes and so on. By setting a bank of these up to resonate at various wind speeds, we did actually generate electricity that could be measured and stored in a capacitor or battery - just not very much of it in terms of energy density for the size of the equipment! It was another interesting diversion from our regular work, and with the advances made in batteries and electronics, it might bear re-examining. It was not what we thought of as a "serious" experiment, 25 years ago, but it was an interesting diversion.

Sadly this won't work. The Muon only has a lifetime of a fraction of a second before it decays, so to get constant power out of a Muon Catalysed Fusion reactor you would need a constant stream of freshly generated Muons. And producing the Muons is very energy intensive. Even though the Muon is short lived  it can still catalyse around 200 fusion reactions which is pretty good!
There have been some estimates I believe that suggest that we *could* perhaps get net power out of a Muon CF reactor, however it wouldn't be economic because you'd need to reinvest so much gross output power back in to keep the reaction going that the net power output would be way too small to be feasible.

author: @mikegofton1
text:
Fusion is a quantum mechanical effect. 
It seems strange that we are continuing to invest large amounts of capital into hot fusion, which is a brute force way of emulating the Sun.
Why not focus on modifying the wave function of the reactants ? - this enables muon catalysed fusion to work at room temperature. The muon ‘shields’ the repulsive force between protons in the nucleus (coulomb barrier). Metal lattice enabled fusion takes a similar approach.
Sure, these techniques still don’t have a total Q exceeding 1, but neither does Tokamak fusion after 60 years and countless billions in funding.

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
It's called the energy  packing fraction, it's also known as nuclear binding energy.  When lighter elements are fused together to make slightly heaver elements. The mass of the elements produced in fusion, together with any atomic particles produced, such as free neutrons,  is slightly less then the element that were combined in the fusion process. This mass is converted into energy. 
At the other end of the periodic table, when a heavy elements splits apart in nuclear fission. Again the total mass of the lighter elements produced along atomic particles produced, is slightly less than the initial mass of  the heavy element that was split. 
The this mass is converted into energy according to Einstein equation, E=mc^2. 
There are diagrams that plot  the packing fraction energy against atomic mass number. The curve shows a sharp fall at the lighter end of the periodic table and a slightly less of a decline at the heavy end. 
The minimum is at iron 56. So all elements decay to become Fe56.
It defines the point where stars, much bigger than our sun, stop burning nuclear fuel. Once their elements have been converted to iron, no more energy is available, either by  fusion or fission. 
I suggest you Google the plot under nuclear packing fraction, or nuclear binding energy.

author: @michaelperrone3867
text: There are two very viable paths forward: you can create heavy electron quasiparticles in some materials that will behave just like muons, enabling fusion without expending the enormous energies to create the muons. You can also go along with lattice confinement fusion and add a radionuclide like tritium as a bremstrahlung X ray source instead of providing artificially produced X rays, enabling self-heating, which is probably what NASA wants to do to replace RTGs. It's also possible to add long-lived nuclear isomers of hafnium or tantalum, and induce their decay with coherent phonons via Mossbauer Fluorescence or (See hafnium controversy) or with X-rays via stimulated emission. When properly done, any of these can cause self-heating of a material. The other challenge is then high pressure high temperature operation with hydrogen, because the hydrogen will want to diffuse out. All in all a lot more feasible than Tokamak fusion though.

author: @t00by00zer
text:
The idea that the sun is powered by gravitationally contained fusion at the core is exactly what has caused the absolute lack of success in producing fusion on earth.
The only place hot enough at the sun for fusion to take place is in the atmosphere where there are a lot of ions available to fuse with the free protons.  The sun's atmosphere is in continuous electrical discharge, a process that causes elemental transmutation right here on earth.  The heat of fusion we feel isn't from hydrogen to helium, but more likely things like carbon to nitrogen.

author: @technosaurus3805
text: My theory with the original Pons and Fleishman experiments was that they overlooked a confounding factor that enabled the fusion reactions. A dopant in the lattice structure in the paladium that could easily migrate along lines to force the heavy hydrogen closer together  or a malfunctioning electronic instrument that caused slightly out of phase resonance between lattice layers pushing the heavy hydrogen together periodically but with increasing magnitude until fusion occured... The same could be explained by extremely high frequency alternating current where the elecrons/holes themselves cause the fluctuations of the hydrogen isotopes.

replies.text: Or as Clif High said only certain electrodes would always either work or not work. The structured atom model explains theres is an orientation to the atomic nucleus, therefore logically it could be that only a range of specific orientations can make it viable. We have no technology to test orientation of atomic nuclei in solid metal, nor to control it when making things out of it.

replies.author: @technosaurus3805
replies.text: Now consider the work done using sound waves to move objects and apply it to electromagnetic waves to move atoms. If you can induce a lattice flow towards a central point, you have combined inertial, magnetic and lattice confinement. We have the math and the models to simulate this now (*well - we did in 2005 when I got my nuclear engineering degree) and may discover that other, cheaper materials may be more appropriate.

replies.author: @technosaurus3805
replies.text:
@635574 thanks to the recent extensive work with graphene, we are studying the effect of rotating layers by N degrees.
I'm not saying the multi-billions research industry surrounding magnetic confinement tokomaks or inertial confinement laser arrays should  abandon their life's work just yet, but a PhD student could definitely attempt to model the alternative at this point. Fusion research is already an extremely multidisciplinary field, but this will take a PhD worth of interdisciplinary collaboration or a decade of personal research and development.

author: @WillHuw

Very interesting Robert.  Have you by any chance come across an experiment that the discoverers are asking everyone and anyone to replicate for themselves (it is said to be very replicable), which consists of a metal plate that they load with Hydrogen via an iron-plating process, and an aluminum plate? They are held in very close proximity but must have a gas gap (which can just be air) between them. This apparently produces a small current that should not be there. They say they have ruled out galvanic and photovoltaic effects and the hypothesis is that it is an LENR effect.
a ytube search for "Breakthrough in measurable LENR / Coldfusion" should find the video.
